text:
I had created a system similar to this - <https://github.com/autosoft-dev/docly> We were a bit early than the market. The market is starting to shape up with GH Co-Pilot and some others (like this one) I strongly believe that coding will be a augmented activity in the future involving both sensible inputs from the computer and the human.

Do you know who created this app? Would be very interested to talk

text: No video from me, I haven't yet figured out how to do screen recordings under Linux (which I switched to from macOS in October). Just written feedback.

elements.text: No video from me, I haven't yet figured out how to do screen recordings under Linux (which I switched to from macOS in October). Just written feedback.

text:
Following the instructions blindly failed pretty quickly: "make linux" stopped because it didn't find gcc. Normal, since I do all serious work with Guix packages (even though the host system is Ubuntu 20.04). So: better list the dependencies in the instructions!
A quick look at the Nix script suggested what I needed, and I ended up doing
guix shell -D lua openssl -- make linux
That makes an environment containing all packages required to compile lua, plus openssl.

elements.text:
Following the instructions blindly failed pretty quickly: "make linux" stopped because it didn't find gcc. Normal, since I do all serious work with Guix packages (even though the host system is Ubuntu 20.04). So: better list the dependencies in the instructions!
A quick look at the Nix script suggested what I needed, and I ended up doing
guix shell -D lua openssl -- make linux
That makes an environment containing all packages required to compile lua, plus openssl.

text: Figuring out the counter wasn't hard, but trying to edit it turned out to be tough: as the screenshot shows, there's a color problem that makes the text in the fields invisible. Started from Ubuntu's default terminal, which uses a dark theme.

elements.text: Figuring out the counter wasn't hard, but trying to edit it turned out to be tough: as the screenshot shows, there's a color problem that makes the text in the fields invisible. Started from Ubuntu's default terminal, which uses a dark theme.

text: Some guy called mark from NZ. He has a “Network with me” link on the bottom of the site.

elements.text: Some guy called mark from NZ. He has a “Network with me” link on the bottom of the site.

text: I had the screen in the instructions, so I typed "main" to continue. I got an editor, but neither "delete" nor "backspace" works, so I couldn't do what I wanted (change the numbers in the initial loop). "Delete" insets "J" on my machine, "backspace" inserts "?" with inverted colors.

elements.text: I had the screen in the instructions, so I typed "main" to continue. I got an editor, but neither "delete" nor "backspace" works, so I couldn't do what I wanted (change the numbers in the initial loop). "Delete" insets "J" on my machine, "backspace" inserts "?" with inverted colors.

text: I have a theory that every HCI researcher eventually either lands up in design philosophy or design ethics :smile:

elements.text: I have a theory that every HCI researcher eventually either lands up in design philosophy or design ethics

text: Without any way to get the source code back into something correct, I went for "abort" (^C), which exits Teliva completely, no questions asked. But then, the title did say "brutalist" :wink:

elements.text: Without any way to get the source code back into something correct, I went for "abort" (^C), which exits Teliva completely, no questions asked. But then, the title did say "brutalist"

text: Of course I opened counter.tlv in Emacs in order to fix my mess, but to my surprise I found a lot more stuff in there than just the Lua code, and preferred to give up - downloading a fresh copy looked easier. Good old git habits :wink:

elements.text: Of course I opened counter.tlv in Emacs in order to fix my mess, but to my surprise I found a lot more stuff in there than just the Lua code, and preferred to give up - downloading a fresh copy looked easier. Good old git habits

text: Inserting "hello world" succeeded at first try. Lucky for me, since without being able to delete text, experimenting would have been hard!

elements.text: Inserting "hello world" succeeded at first try. Lucky for me, since without being able to delete text, experimenting would have been hard!

text: Task 2 looks impossible without deleting a character, but I did succeed to make the counter jump by 10.

elements.text: Task 2 looks impossible without deleting a character, but I did succeed to make the counter jump by 10.

text: Task 2 is easier than Task 1, so from a pedagogical point of view, it's perhaps better to invert them.

elements.text: Task 2 is easier than Task 1, so from a pedagogical point of view, it's perhaps better to invert them.

text: Would have loved to tackle the Towers of Hanoi, but with the "big picture" screen illegible, this looks hopeless.

elements.text: Would have loved to tackle the Towers of Hanoi, but with the "big picture" screen illegible, this looks hopeless.

text: End of report!

elements.text: End of report!

text:
Thanks a lot <@UNGNT3BKR>, <@UML4ZEKDK> and <@UJBAJNFLK>! My first lesson here is that I need to include a list of known issues in the Readme. Backspace/delete handling was a known issue; sorry to waste your time, Konrad. I knew there was no way an international keyboard was going to work. I don't know what smarts I need on top of ncurses, but I think I'll pause features to first get this right.

I'd hoped I'd fixed the color issues the last time they came up, but clearly they still need testing with a wider variety of terminals. iTerm2 is an obvious miss since it's so ubiquitous. I'll also include the Nix/Guix instructions in the Readme; thanks to <@UBN9AFS0N> for creating them for the jam.

Now to go watch <@UNGNT3BKR>'s video..

text: The `?` in reverse video indicates that backspace on your keyboard is emitting `^?`, which is <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delete_character|the >`delete`<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delete_character| keycode in ASCII>. I thought only Macs did that.. :thinking_face:

text:
I resisted learning about these long-tail compatibility issues for years -- which is how I ended up up the Mu tree :cry: I need to just gear up and get through them.

_Edit:_ (By long tail I mean not that they're unimportant, just that there's a combinatorial explosion of cases here, spanning OS, terminal emulator, color scheme and keyboard)

text:
This is funny. How do they represent, e.g., `1001`? Still stacked?

Seems like it would undermine some of the utility of the Hindu–Arabic numeral system.  -- I hope someone turns up the link :slightly_smiling_face:

text: <@U02NTD045HR> I forget. My hazy memory is that it was a tool for back of the envelope comparisons rather than precision calculations, and so only multiples of 1,000 were in the system, but I could be misremembering.
